sourceQuestion:
Which one of the options given below refers to the degree (or arity) of a relation in relational database systems?

A. Number of attributes of its relation schema.
B. Number of tuples stored in the relation.
C. Number of entries in the relation.
D. Number of distinct domains of its relation schema.
sourceAnswer: A. Number of attributes of its relation schema.

parsed.stem: Which one of the options given below refers to the degree (or arity) of a relation in relational database systems?

options.text: Number of attributes of its relation schema.
Number of tuples stored in the relation.
Number of entries in the relation.
Number of distinct domains of its relation schema.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two relations, named Customer and Person, in a database:
  Person ( aadhaar CHAR(12) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(32) )
  Customer ( name VARCHAR(32), phone CHAR(10), aadhaar CHAR(12),
             FOREIGN KEY (aadhaar) REFERENCES Person(aadhaar) )

Which one of the following statements is/are correct?

A. aadhaar is a candidate key in the Customer relation.
B. phone can be NULL in the Customer relation.
C. aadhaar is a candidate key in the Person relation.
D. aadhaar can be NULL in the Customer relation.
sourceAnswer: B, C, D

parsed.stem:
Consider the following two relations, named Customer and Person, in a database:
  Person ( aadhaar CHAR(12) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(32) )
  Customer ( name VARCHAR(32), phone CHAR(10), aadhaar CHAR(12),
             FOREIGN KEY (aadhaar) REFERENCES Person(aadhaar) )

Which one of the following statements is/are correct?

options.text: aadhaar is a candidate key in the Customer relation.
phone can be NULL in the Customer relation.
aadhaar is a candidate key in the Person relation.
aadhaar can be NULL in the Customer relation.

sourceQuestion:
Describe the relational algebraic expression giving the relation returned by the following SQL query.

Select SNAME
from S
Where SNO in
  (select SNO
   from SP
   where PNO in
     (select PNO
      from P
      Where COLOUR='BLUE'))

A. pi_SNAME( S |x| SP |x| sigma_{COLOUR='BLUE'}(P) )
B. pi_SNAME( sigma_{COLOUR='BLUE'}(S x SP x P) )
C. pi_SNAME( S |x| (SP |x| sigma_{COLOUR='BLUE'}(P)) )
D. pi_SNAME( sigma_{COLOUR='BLUE'}(S) |x| SP |x| P )
sourceAnswer: C. pi_SNAME( S |x| (SP |x| sigma_{COLOUR='BLUE'}(P)) )

parsed.stem:
Describe the relational algebraic expression giving the relation returned by the following SQL query.

Select SNAME
from S
Where SNO in
  (select SNO
   from SP
   where PNO in
     (select PNO
      from P
      Where COLOUR='BLUE'))

options.text: pi_SNAME( S |x| SP |x| sigma_{COLOUR='BLUE'}(P) )
pi_SNAME( sigma_{COLOUR='BLUE'}(S x SP x P) )
pi_SNAME( S |x| (SP |x| sigma_{COLOUR='BLUE'}(P)) )
pi_SNAME( sigma_{COLOUR='BLUE'}(S) |x| SP |x| P )

sourceQuestion:
Select SNAME
from S
Where SNO in
  (select SNO
   from SP
   where PNO in
     (select PNO
      from P
      Where COLOUR='BLUE'))

What relations are being used in the above SQL query? Give at least two attributes of each of these relations.

A. S (SNO, SNAME); SP (SNO, PNO); P (PNO, COLOUR)
B. S (SNO, SNAME); SP (SNO, PNAME); P (PNO, COLOUR)
C. S (SNO, SNAME); SP (SNO, PNO); P (COLOUR, PNAME)
D. S (SNAME, SCITY); SP (SNO, PNO); P (PNO, COLOUR)
sourceAnswer: A. S (SNO, SNAME); SP (SNO, PNO); P (PNO, COLOUR)

parsed.stem:

from S
Where SNO in
  (select SNO
   from SP

options.text: S (SNO, SNAME); SP (SNO, PNO); P (PNO, COLOUR)
S (SNO, SNAME); SP (SNO, PNAME); P (PNO, COLOUR)
S (SNO, SNAME); SP (SNO, PNO); P (COLOUR, PNAME)
S (SNAME, SCITY); SP (SNO, PNO); P (PNO, COLOUR)

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relational database:
  employees (eno, ename, address, basic-salary)
  projects (pno, pname, nos-of-staffs-allotted)
  working (pno, eno, pjob)

The queries regarding data in the above database are formulated below in SQL. Describe in ENGLISH sentences the two queries that have been posted:

i.  SELECT ename
    FROM employees
    WHERE eno IN
      (SELECT eno
       FROM working
       GROUP BY eno
       HAVING COUNT(*) > 1)

ii. SELECT ename
    FROM employees
    WHERE eno NOT IN
      (SELECT eno
       FROM working)

A. (i) Names of employees working on more than one project; (ii) Names of employees not assigned to any project
B. (i) Names of employees working on exactly one project; (ii) Names of employees assigned to all projects
C. (i) Names of employees working on more than one project; (ii) Names of employees working on all projects
D. (i) Names of employees with more than one job; (ii) Names of employees not in the working table
sourceAnswer: A. (i) Names of employees working on more than one project; (ii) Names of employees not assigned to any project

parsed.stem:
Consider the following relational database:
  employees (eno, ename, address, basic-salary)
  projects (pno, pname, nos-of-staffs-allotted)
  working (pno, eno, pjob)

The queries regarding data in the above database are formulated below in SQL. Describe in ENGLISH sentences the two queries that have been posted:

i.  SELECT ename
    FROM employees
    WHERE eno IN
      (SELECT eno
       FROM working
       GROUP BY eno
       HAVING COUNT(*) > 1)

ii. SELECT ename
    FROM employees
    WHERE eno NOT IN
      (SELECT eno
       FROM working)

options.text: (i) Names of employees working on more than one project; (ii) Names of employees not assigned to any project
(i) Names of employees working on exactly one project; (ii) Names of employees assigned to all projects
(i) Names of employees working on more than one project; (ii) Names of employees working on all projects
(i) Names of employees with more than one job; (ii) Names of employees not in the working table

sourceQuestion:
Suppose a database consists of the following relations:
SUPPLIER (SCODE, SNAME, CITY)
PART (PCODE, PNAME, PDESC, CITY)
PROJECTS (PRCODE, PRNAME, PRCITY)
SPPR (SCODE, PCODE, PRCODE, QTY)

Write SQL programs corresponding to the following queries:
i. Print PCODE values for parts supplied to any project in DELHI by a supplier in DELHI.
ii. Print all triples <CITY, PCODE, CITY> such that a supplier in the first city supplies the specified part to a project in the second city, but do not print the triples in which the two CITY values are the same.

Which of the following SQL queries correctly answers query (i)?
A. SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, SUPPLIER, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.SCODE = SUPPLIER.SCODE AND SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND SUPPLIER.CITY = 'DELHI' AND PROJECTS.PRCITY = 'DELHI'
B. SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR WHERE SCODE IN (SELECT SCODE FROM SUPPLIER WHERE CITY = 'DELHI')
C. SELECT PCODE FROM PART WHERE CITY = 'DELHI'
D. SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND PRCITY = 'DELHI'
sourceAnswer: A. SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, SUPPLIER, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.SCODE = SUPPLIER.SCODE AND SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND SUPPLIER.CITY = 'DELHI' AND PROJECTS.PRCITY = 'DELHI'

parsed.stem:
Suppose a database consists of the following relations:
SUPPLIER (SCODE, SNAME, CITY)
PART (PCODE, PNAME, PDESC, CITY)
PROJECTS (PRCODE, PRNAME, PRCITY)
SPPR (SCODE, PCODE, PRCODE, QTY)

Write SQL programs corresponding to the following queries:
i. Print PCODE values for parts supplied to any project in DELHI by a supplier in DELHI.
ii. Print all triples <CITY, PCODE, CITY> such that a supplier in the first city supplies the specified part to a project in the second city, but do not print the triples in which the two CITY values are the same.

Which of the following SQL queries correctly answers query (i)?

options.text: SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, SUPPLIER, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.SCODE = SUPPLIER.SCODE AND SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND SUPPLIER.CITY = 'DELHI' AND PROJECTS.PRCITY = 'DELHI'
SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR WHERE SCODE IN (SELECT SCODE FROM SUPPLIER WHERE CITY = 'DELHI')
SELECT PCODE FROM PART WHERE CITY = 'DELHI'
SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND PRCITY = 'DELHI'

sourceQuestion:
Suppose we have a database consisting of the following three relations:
- FREQUENTS (student, parlor) giving the parlors each student visits.
- SERVES (parlor, ice-cream) indicating what kind of ice-creams each parlor serves.
- LIKES (student, ice-cream) indicating what ice-creams each student likes.

(Assume that each student likes at least one ice-cream and frequents at least one parlor.)

Express the following in SQL:
Find the students that frequent at least one parlor that serves some ice-cream that they like.

Which of the following is correct?
A. SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, SERVES, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.parlor = SERVES.parlor AND SERVES.ice-cream = LIKES.ice-cream AND FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student
B. SELECT student FROM LIKES WHERE ice-cream IN (SELECT ice-cream FROM SERVES)
C. SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS WHERE parlor IN (SELECT parlor FROM SERVES)
D. SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student
sourceAnswer: A. SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, SERVES, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.parlor = SERVES.parlor AND SERVES.ice-cream = LIKES.ice-cream AND FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student

parsed.stem:
Suppose we have a database consisting of the following three relations:
- FREQUENTS (student, parlor) giving the parlors each student visits.
- SERVES (parlor, ice-cream) indicating what kind of ice-creams each parlor serves.
- LIKES (student, ice-cream) indicating what ice-creams each student likes.

(Assume that each student likes at least one ice-cream and frequents at least one parlor.)

Express the following in SQL:
Find the students that frequent at least one parlor that serves some ice-cream that they like.

Which of the following is correct?

options.text: SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, SERVES, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.parlor = SERVES.parlor AND SERVES.ice-cream = LIKES.ice-cream AND FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student
SELECT student FROM LIKES WHERE ice-cream IN (SELECT ice-cream FROM SERVES)
SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS WHERE parlor IN (SELECT parlor FROM SERVES)
SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is/are correct?
A. An SQL query automatically eliminates duplicates
B. An SQL query will not work if there are no indexes on the relations
C. SQL permits attribute names to be repeated in the same relation
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: D. None of the above

parsed.stem: Which of the following is/are correct?

options.text: An SQL query automatically eliminates duplicates
An SQL query will not work if there are no indexes on the relations
SQL permits attribute names to be repeated in the same relation
None of the above

sourceQuestion:
Given relations r(w, x) and s(y, z), the result of

  SELECT DISTINCT w, x FROM r, s

is guaranteed to be the same as r, provided:
A. r has no duplicates and s is non-empty
B. r and s have no duplicates
C. s has no duplicates and r is non-empty
D. r and s have the same number of tuples
sourceAnswer: A. r has no duplicates and s is non-empty

parsed.stem:
Given relations r(w, x) and s(y, z), the result of

  SELECT DISTINCT w, x FROM r, s

is guaranteed to be the same as r, provided:

options.text: r has no duplicates and s is non-empty
r and s have no duplicates
s has no duplicates and r is non-empty
r and s have the same number of tuples

sourceQuestion:
In SQL, relations can contain null values, and comparisons with null values are treated as unknown. Suppose all comparisons with a null value are treated as false. Which of the following pairs is not equivalent?
A. x = 5 and not(x = 5) ; not(x = 5)
B. x = 5 and x > 4 and x < 6 ; x = 5, where x is an integer
C. x != 5 ; not(x = 5)
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: C. x != 5 ; not(x = 5)

parsed.stem: In SQL, relations can contain null values, and comparisons with null values are treated as unknown. Suppose all comparisons with a null value are treated as false. Which of the following pairs is not equivalent?

options.text: x = 5 and not(x = 5) ; not(x = 5)
x = 5 and x > 4 and x < 6 ; x = 5, where x is an integer
None of the above

sourceQuestion:
Consider a bank database with only one relation:
transaction (transno, acctno, date, amount)
The amount attribute value is positive for deposits and negative for withdrawals.
a. Define an SQL view TP containing the information (acctno, T1.date, T2.amount) for every pair of transactions T1, T2 such that T1 and T2 are on the same account and the date of T2 is <= the date of T1 (i.e., T2 occurred before or on T1).
b. Using only the above view TP, find for each account the minimum balance it ever reached (not including the 0 balance when the account is created). Assume there is at most one transaction per day on each account and each account has at least one transaction since it was created. To simplify your query, break it up into 2 steps by defining an intermediate view V.

Which of the following is the correct SQL view definition for TP?
A. CREATE VIEW TP AS SELECT T1.acctno, T1.date, T2.amount FROM transaction T1, transaction T2 WHERE T1.acctno = T2.acctno AND T2.date <= T1.date
B. CREATE VIEW TP AS SELECT T1.acctno, T1.date, T2.amount FROM transaction T1, transaction T2 WHERE T1.acctno != T2.acctno AND T2.date <= T1.date
C. CREATE VIEW TP AS SELECT T1.acctno, T1.date, T2.amount FROM transaction T1, transaction T2 WHERE T1.acctno = T2.acctno AND T2.date >= T1.date
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: A. CREATE VIEW TP AS SELECT T1.acctno, T1.date, T2.amount FROM transaction T1, transaction T2 WHERE T1.acctno = T2.acctno AND T2.date <= T1.date

parsed.stem:
Consider a bank database with only one relation:
transaction (transno, acctno, date, amount)
The amount attribute value is positive for deposits and negative for withdrawals.
a. Define an SQL view TP containing the information (acctno, T1.date, T2.amount) for every pair of transactions T1, T2 such that T1 and T2 are on the same account and the date of T2 is <= the date of T1 (i.e., T2 occurred before or on T1).
b. Using only the above view TP, find for each account the minimum balance it ever reached (not including the 0 balance when the account is created). Assume there is at most one transaction per day on each account and each account has at least one transaction since it was created. To simplify your query, break it up into 2 steps by defining an intermediate view V.

Which of the following is the correct SQL view definition for TP?

options.text: CREATE VIEW TP AS SELECT T1.acctno, T1.date, T2.amount FROM transaction T1, transaction T2 WHERE T1.acctno = T2.acctno AND T2.date <= T1.date
CREATE VIEW TP AS SELECT T1.acctno, T1.date, T2.amount FROM transaction T1, transaction T2 WHERE T1.acctno != T2.acctno AND T2.date <= T1.date
CREATE VIEW TP AS SELECT T1.acctno, T1.date, T2.amount FROM transaction T1, transaction T2 WHERE T1.acctno = T2.acctno AND T2.date >= T1.date
None of the above

sourceQuestion:
Consider a relation geq which represents 'greater than or equal to', that is, (a, b) in geq only if a >= b.

create table geq (
  lb integer not null,
  ub integer not null,
  primary key (lb),
  foreign key (ub) references geq(lb) on delete cascade
);

Which of the following is possible if tuple (x, y) is deleted?
A. A tuple (z, w) with z > x is deleted
B. A tuple (z, w) with z = x is deleted
C. A tuple (z, w) with w < y is deleted
D. The deletion of (x, y) is prohibited
sourceAnswer: C. A tuple (z, w) with w < y is deleted

parsed.stem:
Consider a relation geq which represents 'greater than or equal to', that is, (a, b) in geq only if a >= b.

create table geq (
  lb integer not null,
  ub integer not null,
  primary key (lb),
  foreign key (ub) references geq(lb) on delete cascade
);

Which of the following is possible if tuple (x, y) is deleted?

options.text: A tuple (z, w) with z > x is deleted
A tuple (z, w) with z = x is deleted
A tuple (z, w) with w < y is deleted
The deletion of (x, y) is prohibited

sourceQuestion:
Consider the set of relations shown below and the SQL query that follows.

Students: (Roll_number, Name, Date_of_birth)
Courses: (Course_number, Course_name, Instructor)
Grades: (Roll_number, Course_number, Grade)

Select distinct Name
from Students, Courses, Grades
where Students.Roll_number = Grades.Roll_number
and Courses.Course_number = Grades.Course_number
and Courses.Instructor = 'Korth'
and Grades.Grade = 'A'

Which of the following sets is computed by the above query?

A. Names of students who have got an A grade in all courses taught by Korth
B. Names of students who have got an A grade in all courses
C. Names of students who have got an A grade in at least one of the courses taught by Korth
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: C. Names of students who have got an A grade in at least one of the courses taught by Korth

parsed.stem:
Consider the set of relations shown below and the SQL query that follows.

Students: (Roll_number, Name, Date_of_birth)
Courses: (Course_number, Course_name, Instructor)
Grades: (Roll_number, Course_number, Grade)

Select distinct Name
from Students, Courses, Grades
where Students.Roll_number = Grades.Roll_number
and Courses.Course_number = Grades.Course_number
and Courses.Instructor = 'Korth'
and Grades.Grade = 'A'

Which of the following sets is computed by the above query?

options.text: Names of students who have got an A grade in all courses taught by Korth
Names of students who have got an A grade in all courses
Names of students who have got an A grade in at least one of the courses taught by Korth
None of the above

sourceQuestion:
The employee information in a company is stored in the relation
Employee(name, sex, salary, deptName)

Consider the following SQL query:

Select deptName
From Employee
Where sex = 'M'
Group by deptName
Having avg(salary) > (select avg(salary) from Employee)

It returns the names of the department in which

A. the average salary is more than the average salary in the company
B. the average salary of male employees is more than the average salary of all male employees in the company
C. the average salary of male employees is more than the average salary of all employees in the company
D. the average salary of male employees is more than the average salary of male employees in the same department
sourceAnswer: C. the average salary of male employees is more than the average salary of all employees in the company

parsed.stem:
The employee information in a company is stored in the relation
Employee(name, sex, salary, deptName)

Consider the following SQL query:

Select deptName
From Employee

Group by deptName

It returns the names of the department in which

options.text: the average salary is more than the average salary in the company
the average salary of male employees is more than the average salary of all male employees in the company
the average salary of male employees is more than the average salary of all employees in the company
the average salary of male employees is more than the average salary of male employees in the same department

sourceQuestion:
The relation book (title, price) contains the titles and prices of different books. Assuming that no two books have the same price, what does the following SQL query list?

select title
from book as B
where (select count(*)
       from book as T
       where T.price > B.price) < 5

A. Titles of the four most expensive books
B. Title of the fifth most inexpensive book
C. Titles of the five most expensive books
D. Title of the fifth most expensive book
sourceAnswer: C. Titles of the five most expensive books

parsed.stem:
The relation book (title, price) contains the titles and prices of different books. Assuming that no two books have the same price, what does the following SQL query list?

select title
from book as B
where (select count(*)
       from book as T
       where T.price > B.price) < 5

options.text: Titles of the four most expensive books
Title of the fifth most inexpensive book
Titles of the five most expensive books
Title of the fifth most expensive book

sourceQuestion:
Consider the relation account (customer, balance) where the customer is a primary key and there are no null values. We would like to rank customers according to decreasing balance. The customer with the largest balance gets rank 1. Ties are not broken but ranks are skipped: if exactly two customers have the largest balance, both get rank 1 and rank 3 is the next rank assigned. Consider the following SQL queries:

Query1:
select A.customer, 1 + count(B.customer)
from account A, account B
where A.balance < B.balance
group by A.customer

Query2:
select A.customer, 1 + count(B.customer)
from account A, account B
where A.balance <= B.balance
group by A.customer

Consider these statements about Query1 and Query2.
1. Query1 will produce the same set as Query2 for some but not all databases.
2. Query1 and Query2 are a correct implementation of the specification.
3. Query1 is a correct implementation of the specification but Query2 is not.
4. Neither Query1 nor Query2 is a correct implementation of the specification by Query2 is a correct relational query. Assigning ranks using a relational query takes less time than scanning in decreasing balance order satisfying ODBC.

Which two of the above statements are correct?

A. 2 and 5
B. 1 and 3
C. 1 and 4
D. 3 and 5
sourceAnswer: B. 1 and 3

parsed.stem:
Consider the relation account (customer, balance) where the customer is a primary key and there are no null values. We would like to rank customers according to decreasing balance. The customer with the largest balance gets rank 1. Ties are not broken but ranks are skipped: if exactly two customers have the largest balance, both get rank 1 and rank 3 is the next rank assigned. Consider the following SQL queries:

Query1:
select A.customer, 1 + count(B.customer)
from account A, account B
where A.balance < B.balance
group by A.customer

Query2:
select A.customer, 1 + count(B.customer)
from account A, account B
where A.balance <= B.balance
group by A.customer

Consider these statements about Query1 and Query2.
1. Query1 will produce the same set as Query2 for some but not all databases.
2. Query1 and Query2 are a correct implementation of the specification.
3. Query1 is a correct implementation of the specification but Query2 is not.
4. Neither Query1 nor Query2 is a correct implementation of the specification by Query2 is a correct relational query. Assigning ranks using a relational query takes less time than scanning in decreasing balance order satisfying ODBC.

Which two of the above statements are correct?

options.text: 2 and 5
1 and 3
1 and 4
3 and 5

sourceQuestion:
Consider the relation enrolled (student, course) in which (student, course) is the primary key, and the relation paid (student, amount) where student is the primary key. Assume no null values and no foreign keys or integrity constraints. Given the following four queries:

Query1:
select student from enrolled where student in (select student from paid)

Query2:
select student from paid where student in (select student from enrolled)

Query3:
select E.student from enrolled E, paid P where E.student = P.student

Query4:
select student from paid where exists (select * from enrolled where enrolled.student = paid.student) - (select student from enrolled where amount > 0)

Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. All queries produce identical row sets for any database
B. Query1 and Query2 are a correct implementation of the specification
C. Query1 and Query2 produce different row sets for all databases
D. There exist databases for which Query4 will encounter an integrity violation at runtime
sourceAnswer: A. All queries produce identical row sets for any database

parsed.stem:
Consider the relation enrolled (student, course) in which (student, course) is the primary key, and the relation paid (student, amount) where student is the primary key. Assume no null values and no foreign keys or integrity constraints. Given the following four queries:

Query1:
select student from enrolled where student in (select student from paid)

Query2:
select student from paid where student in (select student from enrolled)

Query3:
select E.student from enrolled E, paid P where E.student = P.student

Query4:
select student from paid where exists (select * from enrolled where enrolled.student = paid.student) - (select student from enrolled where amount > 0)

Which one of the following statements is correct?

options.text: All queries produce identical row sets for any database
Query1 and Query2 are a correct implementation of the specification
Query1 and Query2 produce different row sets for all databases
There exist databases for which Query4 will encounter an integrity violation at runtime

sourceQuestion:
Consider the relation enrolled(student, course) in which (student, course) is the primary key, and the relation paid(student, amount) where student is the primary key. Assume no null values and no foreign keys or integrity constraints. Assume that amounts 6000, 7000, 9000, and 10000 were each paid by 20% of the students. Consider these query plans (Plan 1 on left, Plan 2 on right) to 'list all courses taken by students who have paid more than x'.

Plan 1:
Select student from paid where amount > x
For each such student s:
  Select course from enrolled where student = s

Plan 2:
Select distinct course from enrolled
For each course c:
  Select student from enrolled where course = c
  For each such student s:
    Check if s is in the result of: Select student from paid where amount > x

A disk seek takes 4ms, disk data transfer bandwidth is 300 MB/s and checking a tuple to see if amount is greater than x takes 10 microseconds. For x = 9000, which of the following statements is correct?

A. Plan 1 and Plan 2 will not output identical row sets for all databases
B. For Query2 and Query1 return identical row sets for all databases but there exist databases for which Query3 and Query2 return different row sets
C. For x = 9000, Plan 1 executes faster than Plan 2 for some databases
D. For x = 9000, Plan 1 executes slower than Plan 2 for all databases
sourceAnswer: C. For x = 9000, Plan 1 executes faster than Plan 2 for some databases

Consider the relation enrolled(student, course) in which (student, course) is the primary key, and the relation paid(student, amount) where student is the primary key. Assume no null values and no foreign keys or integrity constraints. Assume that amounts 6000, 7000, 9000, and 10000 were each paid by 20% of the students. Consider these query plans (Plan 1 on left, Plan 2 on right) to 'list all courses taken by students who have paid more than x'.

Plan 1:
Select student from paid where amount > x
For each such student s:
  Select course from enrolled where student = s

Plan 2:
Select distinct course from enrolled
For each course c:
  Select student from enrolled where course = c
  For each such student s:
    Check if s is in the result of: Select student from paid where amount > x

A disk seek takes 4ms, disk data transfer bandwidth is 300 MB/s and checking a tuple to see if amount is greater than x takes 10 microseconds. For x = 9000, which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: Plan 1 and Plan 2 will not output identical row sets for all databases
For Query2 and Query1 return identical row sets for all databases but there exist databases for which Query3 and Query2 return different row sets
For x = 9000, Plan 1 executes faster than Plan 2 for some databases
For x = 9000, Plan 1 executes slower than Plan 2 for all databases

sourceQuestion:
Consider the table employee(empId, name, department, salary) and the two queries below. Assuming that department D5 has more than one employee, and we want to find the employees who get higher salary than anyone in department D5.

Q1: SELECT empId FROM employee WHERE salary > ANY (SELECT salary FROM employee WHERE department = 'D5')

Q2: SELECT empId FROM employee WHERE salary > ALL (SELECT salary FROM employee WHERE department = 'D5')

Which one of the statements is TRUE for any arbitrary employee table?

A. Q1 is the correct query
B. Q2 is the correct query
C. Both Q1 and Q2 produce the same answer
D. Neither Q1 nor Q2 is the correct query
sourceAnswer: B. Q2 is the correct query

Q2: SELECT empId FROM employee WHERE salary > ALL (SELECT salary FROM employee WHERE department = 'D5')

options.text: Q1 is the correct query
Q2 is the correct query
Both Q1 and Q2 produce the same answer
Neither Q1 nor Q2 is the correct query

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relational schema:
Suppliers(sid:integer, sname:string, city:string, street:string)
Parts(pid:integer, pname:string, color:string)
Catalog(sid:integer, pid:integer, cost:real)

Assume that the suppliers relation has no two suppliers from the same city. Also assume each supplier in the Catalog relation supplies at least one part. Consider the following relational query on the above database:

SELECT S.sname
FROM Suppliers S
WHERE S.sid NOT IN
  (SELECT C.sid
   FROM Catalog C
   WHERE C.pid NOT IN
     (SELECT P.pid
      FROM Parts P
      WHERE P.color <> 'blue'))

Assume that the above schema are not empty. Which one of the following is the correct interpretation of the above query?

A. Find the names of all suppliers who have supplied a non-blue part.
B. Find the names of all suppliers who have not supplied a non-blue part.
C. Find the names of all suppliers who have supplied only non-blue parts.
D. Find the names of all suppliers who have not supplied only blue parts.
sourceAnswer: B. Find the names of all suppliers who have not supplied a non-blue part.

options.text: Find the names of all suppliers who have supplied a non-blue part.
Find the names of all suppliers who have not supplied a non-blue part.
Find the names of all suppliers who have supplied only non-blue parts.
Find the names of all suppliers who have not supplied only blue parts.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relational schema:
Suppliers(sid:integer, sname:string, city:string, street:string)
Parts(pid:integer, pname:string, color:string)
Catalog(sid:integer, pid:integer, cost:real)

Assume that the suppliers relation has no two suppliers from the same city. Also assume each supplier in the Catalog relation supplies at least one part. Assume that the above schema are not empty. Which one of the following is TRUE about the above schema?

A. The schema is in BCNF
B. The schema is in 3NF but not in BCNF
C. The schema is in 2NF but not in 3NF
D. The schema is not in 2NF
sourceAnswer: A. The schema is in BCNF

sourceQuestion:
A relational schema for a train reservation database is given below.

passenger(pid, pname, age)
reservation(pid, class, tid)

Consider the following instance of these tables:

Passenger:
pid | pname   | age
 0  | Sachin  | 68
 1  | Rahul   | 66
 2  | Sourav  | 67
 3  | Anil    | 69

Reservation:
pid | class | tid
 0  | AC    | 8200
 1  | SC    | 8201
 2  | AC    | 8203
 3  | SC    | 8202

What pids are returned by the following SQL query for the above instance of the tables?

SELECT pid
FROM Reservation
WHERE class = 'AC'
AND EXISTS (
  SELECT *
  FROM Passenger
  WHERE age > 60
  AND Passenger.pid = Reservation.pid
);

A. 1, 0
B. 1, 2
C. 1, 3
D. 1, 5

reservation(pid, class, tid)

 1  | SC    | 8201

What pids are returned by the following SQL query for the above instance of the tables?

SELECT pid
FROM Reservation
WHERE class = 'AC'
AND EXISTS (
  SELECT *
  FROM Passenger
  WHERE age > 60
  AND Passenger.pid = Reservation.pid
);

sourceQuestion:
Consider a database table T containing two columns X and Y, each of type integer. After the creation of the table, one record (X=1, Y=1) is inserted in the table.

Let MX and MY denote the respective maximum values of X and Y among all records in the table at any point in time. Using MX and MY, new records are inserted in the table as follows:

INSERT INTO T VALUES (MX+1, 2*MY)

This INSERT is performed (MX - 1) * (2*MY + 1) times (i.e., it is repeated that many times in a loop).

What will be the output of the following SQL query after the steps mentioned above are carried out?

SELECT Y FROM T WHERE X = 7

A. 127
B. 255
C. 129
D. 257

parsed.stem:
Consider a database table T containing two columns X and Y, each of type integer. After the creation of the table, one record (X=1, Y=1) is inserted in the table.

Let MX and MY denote the respective maximum values of X and Y among all records in the table at any point in time. Using MX and MY, new records are inserted in the table as follows:

INSERT INTO T VALUES (MX+1, 2*MY)

This INSERT is performed (MX - 1) * (2*MY + 1) times (i.e., it is repeated that many times in a loop).

What will be the output of the following SQL query after the steps mentioned above are carried out?

SELECT Y FROM T WHERE X = 7

sourceQuestion:
Database table by name Loan_Records is given below:

Borrower    | Bank_Manager | Loan_Amount
------------|--------------|------------
Ramesh      | Suderajan    | 10000.00
Suresh      | Rasaggopal   | 5000.00
Mahesh      | Suderajan    | 7000.00

What is the output of the following SQL query?

SELECT count(*)
FROM (
  SELECT Borrower, Bank_Manager FROM Loan_Records
) AS S
NATURAL JOIN
(
  SELECT Bank_Manager, Loan_Amount FROM Loan_Records
) AS T
);

A. 3
B. 9
C. 5
D. 6

parsed.stem:
Database table by name Loan_Records is given below:

Borrower    | Bank_Manager | Loan_Amount
------------|--------------|------------
Ramesh      | Suderajan    | 10000.00
Suresh      | Rasaggopal   | 5000.00
Mahesh      | Suderajan    | 7000.00

What is the output of the following SQL query?

SELECT count(*)
FROM (
  SELECT Borrower, Bank_Manager FROM Loan_Records
) AS S
NATURAL JOIN
(
  SELECT Bank_Manager, Loan_Amount FROM Loan_Records
) AS T
);

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements are TRUE about an SQL query?

P : An SQL query can contain a HAVING clause even if it does not have a GROUP BY clause.
Q : An SQL query can contain a HAVING clause only if it has a GROUP BY clause.
R : All attributes used in the GROUP BY clause must appear in the SELECT clause.
S : Not all attributes used in the GROUP BY clause need to appear in the SELECT clause.

A. P and R
B. P and S
C. Q and R
D. Q and S
sourceAnswer: B. P and S

parsed.stem:
Which of the following statements are TRUE about an SQL query?

P : An SQL query can contain a HAVING clause even if it does not have a GROUP BY clause.
Q : An SQL query can contain a HAVING clause only if it has a GROUP BY clause.
R : All attributes used in the GROUP BY clause must appear in the SELECT clause.
S : Not all attributes used in the GROUP BY clause need to appear in the SELECT clause.

options.text: P and R
P and S
Q and R
Q and S

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relations A, B and C:

A:
Id | Name  | Age
---|-------|----
12 | Arun  | 60
15 | Shreya| 24
99 | Rohit | 11

B:
Id | Name  | Age
---|-------|----
15 | Shreya| 24
25 | Hari  | 40
99 | Rohit | 11

How many tuples does the result of the following SQL query contain?

SELECT A.Id
FROM A
WHERE A.Age > ALL
  (SELECT B.Age
   FROM B
   WHERE B.Name = 'Arun');

A. 4
B. 3
C. 0
D. 1

parsed.stem: Consider the following relations A, B and C:

options.text: Id | Name | Age ---|-------|---- 12 | Arun | 60 15 | Shreya| 24 99 | Rohit | 11

Id | Name | Age ---|-------|---- 15 | Shreya| 24 25 | Hari | 40 99 | Rohit | 11 How many tuples does the result of the following SQL query contain? SELECT A.Id FROM A WHERE A.Age > ALL (SELECT B.Age FROM B WHERE B.Name = 'Arun'); A. 4 B. 3

sourceQuestion:
Given the following statements:
S1: A foreign key declaration can always be replaced by an equivalent check assertion in SQL.
S2: Given the table R(a, b, c) where a and b together form the primary key, the following is a valid table definition.

CREATE TABLE S (
  a INTEGER,
  d INTEGER,
  e INTEGER,
  PRIMARY KEY (d),
  FOREIGN KEY (a) REFERENCES R
);

Which one of the following statements is CORRECT?

A. S1 is TRUE and S2 is FALSE
B. Both S1 and S2 are TRUE
C. S1 is FALSE and S2 is TRUE
D. Both S1 and S2 are FALSE
sourceAnswer: C. S1 is FALSE and S2 is TRUE

parsed.stem:
Given the following statements:
S1: A foreign key declaration can always be replaced by an equivalent check assertion in SQL.
S2: Given the table R(a, b, c) where a and b together form the primary key, the following is a valid table definition.

CREATE TABLE S (
  a INTEGER,
  d INTEGER,
  e INTEGER,
  PRIMARY KEY (d),
  FOREIGN KEY (a) REFERENCES R
);

Which one of the following statements is CORRECT?

options.text: S1 is TRUE and S2 is FALSE
Both S1 and S2 are TRUE
S1 is FALSE and S2 is TRUE
Both S1 and S2 are FALSE

sourceQuestion:
Given the following schema:
employees(emp-id, first-name, last-name, hire-date, dept-id, salary)
departments(dept-id, dept-name, manager-id, location-id)

You want to display the last names and hire dates of all latest hires in their respective departments in the location ID 1700. You issue the following query:

SELECT last-name, hire-date
FROM employees e
WHERE hire-date IN
  (SELECT MAX(hire-date)
   FROM employees
   INNER JOIN departments
   ON (departments.dept-id = emp.dept-id)
   WHERE location-id = 1700
   GROUP BY dept-id);

What is the outcome?

A. It executes but does not give the correct result
B. It executes and gives the correct result
C. It generates an error because of pairwise comparison
D. It generates an error because the GROUP BY clause cannot be used with table joins in a sub-query
sourceAnswer: A. It executes but does not give the correct result

parsed.stem:
Given the following schema:
employees(emp-id, first-name, last-name, hire-date, dept-id, salary)

SELECT last-name, hire-date
FROM employees e
WHERE hire-date IN
  (SELECT MAX(hire-date)
   FROM employees
   INNER JOIN departments
   ON (departments.dept-id = emp.dept-id)
   WHERE location-id = 1700
   GROUP BY dept-id);

What is the outcome?

options.text: It executes but does not give the correct result
It executes and gives the correct result
It generates an error because of pairwise comparison
It generates an error because the GROUP BY clause cannot be used with table joins in a sub-query

sourceQuestion:
SQL allows duplicate tuples in relations, and correspondingly defines the multiplicity of tuples in the result of joins. Which one of the following queries always gives the same answer as the nested query shown below:

select * from R where a in (select S.a from S)

A. select R.* from R, S where R.a = S.a
B. select distinct R.* from R, S where R.a = S.a
C. select R.* from R, (select distinct a from S) as S1 where R.a = S1.a
D. select R.* from R where exists (select S.a from S where R.a = S.a)
sourceAnswer: D. select R.* from R where exists (select S.a from S where R.a = S.a)

parsed.stem:
SQL allows duplicate tuples in relations, and correspondingly defines the multiplicity of tuples in the result of joins. Which one of the following queries always gives the same answer as the nested query shown below:

select * from R where a in (select S.a from S)

options.text: select R.* from R, S where R.a = S.a
select distinct R.* from R, S where R.a = S.a
select R.* from R, (select distinct a from S) as S1 where R.a = S1.a
select R.* from R where exists (select S.a from S where R.a = S.a)

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following relational schema:
employee(empId, empName, empDept)
customer(custId, custName, custRating, custRepId)
salesRepSal is a foreign key in customer referencing empId of employee. Assume that each employee makes a sale to at least one customer. What does the following query return?

SELECT empName FROM employee
WHERE NOT EXISTS
  (SELECT custId FROM customer
   WHERE custRepId = empId
   AND custRating = 'GOOD');

A. Names of all employees with at least one of their customers having a 'GOOD' rating
B. Names of all employees with all of their customers having a 'GOOD' rating
C. Names of all employees with none of their customers having a 'GOOD' rating
D. Names of all employees with at least one of their customers not having a 'GOOD' rating
sourceAnswer: C. Names of all employees with none of their customers having a 'GOOD' rating

SELECT empName FROM employee
WHERE NOT EXISTS
  (SELECT custId FROM customer
   WHERE custRepId = empId
   AND custRating = 'GOOD');

options.text: Names of all employees with at least one of their customers having a 'GOOD' rating
Names of all employees with all of their customers having a 'GOOD' rating
Names of all employees with none of their customers having a 'GOOD' rating
